name: Taro
recipeIngredient: 1/4 cup Chard, chopped (optional) - for dipping sauce (oyster or other)
recipeInstructions:
1. Other names for taro include dasheen, elephant's ear, nampi, arum lily, cocoyam, yautia, malagna, eddo, and kolokassi.
2. Asiatic people typically steam, boil, or stew taro. Hawaiians make poi from it and also stew the leaves.
3. Peel the taro corms and soak them in cold water until ready to cook.
4. Boil the peeled taro corms in water to cover, or steam them in a bamboo steamer until tender.
5. Serve the cooked taro plain as you would boiled potatoes.
6. To season the taro, drain it while still hot and cut into cubes.
7. Combine the cubed taro with chopped chard and crushed garlic.
8. Heat a wok or skillet with just enough vegetable oil to coat the bottom.
9. Stir-fry the taro mixture for 1 minute.
10. Serve hot or cold with dipping sauce, if desired.
1/2 cup of prepared taro (without optional ingredients) contains approximately 92 calories, 1 1/2 starch exchanges, 22 grams carbohydrate, 2 grams protein, 10 mg sodium, and 319 mg potassium. Adapted from Diabetic Cooking from Around the World by V. Chantiles (1989), shared but not tested by Elizabeth Rodier in Jan 94.
